title: How to Use a Spiralizer for Spiralized Vegetables
meta_description: Mastering your kitchen tools starts here. Learn how to use a spiralizer for spiralized vegetables to make delicious, healthy noodles with ease.
html:
If you are looking to cut carbs and add vibrant color to your plate, finding the right spiralizer for spiralized vegetables is a genuine game-changer. It transforms boring produce into long, curly noodles that mimic pasta but offer a fraction of the calories. Whether you are aiming for a strict paleo diet or just want to eat more veggies, this tool is essential.

Choosing the Right Vegetable Spiralizer

Before you start cranking out noodles, it is important to understand the hardware. Generally, you have two main options: handheld models and countertop versions. Handheld spiralizers are compact, affordable, and perfect for small batches or quick zucchini noodles. Countertop models, on the other hand, sit securely with suction cups and usually offer multiple blade options for different noodle shapes.

The Vegetable Spiralizer is designed to handle both needs, offering handheld and countertop spiralizers for low-carb meals. If you are cooking for one, a handheld version is often sufficient. However, if you are prepping meals for a family or want to spiralize harder vegetables like sweet potatoes or butternut squash, the leverage provided by a countertop machine will save your wrist significant strain.

Prepping Your Produce for Success

The secret to perfect noodles lies in the preparation. You cannot just stick a whole, knobby carrot into the machine and expect magic. For the best results, choose vegetables that are straight and at least two inches in diameter. Curvy vegetables will break the ribbon, resulting in short, broken strands rather than long, elegant noodles.

Wash your vegetables thoroughly. If you are using zucchini, you don’t necessarily need to peel it—the green skin adds nice texture and color—but for tough vegetables like beets or carrots, peeling is recommended. Most importantly, cut both ends of the vegetable flat to create a stable surface. If one end is slanted, the blade won't catch evenly, and the vegetable will wobble instead of spinning.

How to Use a Spiralizer for Spiralized Vegetables

Once your veggies are prepped, the actual process is satisfyingly fast. Whether you are using the handheld or countertop version of the Vegetable Spiralizer, the mechanics are similar. Here is the step-by-step method to ensure you get the longest noodles possible:

  1. Secure the Machine: If using a countertop model, press down firmly so the suction cups grip the counter. If handheld, hold the body over a cutting board or bowl.
  2. Insert the Vegetable: Place the flat end of the vegetable against the small coring circle on the blade end. Push the teeth of the handle (or the pushing plate) into the other flat end of the vegetable.
  3. Apply Pressure and Turn: Start turning the handle. For handheld models, you twist the vegetable itself against the blades. For countertop models, you turn the crank handle. Push forward with steady, consistent pressure to feed the veg through the blades.
  4. Watch the Noodles Fall: As the vegetable moves through the blade, noodles will come out the side. Continue until you reach the end of the vegetable or until only the small core (the part attached to the coring circle) remains.

Cooking Your Veggie Noodles

Now that you have a mountain of noodles, how do you cook them? The method depends entirely on the vegetable. Zucchini noodles (zoodles) are mostly water. If you boil them, they will turn into a mushy, watery mess. Instead, sauté them in a pan with a little olive oil for just 2 to 3 minutes. They should still have a slight crunch (al dente).

Harder vegetables like carrots, beets, sweet potatoes, and butternut squash require more heat. You can roast them in the oven at 400°F for about 10 to 15 minutes, or boil them for 5 to 8 minutes. These heartier noodles hold up well to heavy sauces like bolognese or creamy alfredo. Don't be afraid to toss your raw noodles into a salad, either—raw carrot and beet noodles add an incredible crunch that lettuce simply can't match.

Cleaning and Maintenance

Let’s be honest: cleaning a spiralizer can be the worst part of the process if you let the food dry on the blades. As soon as you are finished spiralizing, rinse the blades under warm water. Use a small cleaning brush (often included with your Vegetable Spiralizer) to dislodge any stuck skin or flesh from the sharp teeth.

Never put your fingers near the blades while scrubbing; they are extremely sharp. Check the manufacturer's instructions to see if your specific model is dishwasher safe. While many blades are stainless steel, the high heat of a dishwasher can sometimes dull the edges over time, so hand washing is usually the best way to keep your tool sharp for years.

FAQ

What vegetables work best in a spiralizer?

Firmer vegetables are ideal. Zucchini, yellow squash, carrots, beets, sweet potatoes, butternut squash, cucumbers, and turnips all spiralize beautifully. Avoid very soft vegetables like ripe tomatoes or stringy vegetables like celery, as they will turn to mush instead of noodles.

Do I need to peel the vegetables before spiralizing?

It depends on the vegetable and your preference. For zucchini and cucumber, leaving the skin on adds color and fiber. For tougher skins like carrots, beets, and sweet potatoes, peeling is recommended for a better texture and appearance.

How do I prevent zucchini noodles from getting soggy?

After spiralizing, toss your zucchini noodles with a pinch of salt and let them sit in a colander for 10 to 15 minutes. The salt draws out excess moisture. Squeeze them gently with a paper towel before cooking to remove the released water.
